title: Elastic Moduli
subtitle: Young's modulus, Bulk modulus and Modulus of Rigidity for Class 11 Physics (NCERT)

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
The ratio of stress to strain defines a material's elastic modulus — a measure of stiffness. There are three moduli, one for each type of deformation:
Young's modulus (Y): Resistance to stretching/compression along one direction
Shear modulus (G): Resistance to shearing (tangential) deformation
Bulk modulus (B): Resistance to uniform compression from all sides
A higher modulus = stiffer material = less deformation for the same stress.

blocks.heading: Young's Modulus:
blocks.body:
Shear Modulus:
Bulk Modulus:
(Negative sign because volume decreases when pressure increases.)
Compressibility = 1/B.
Typical values: Y_{\text{steel}} \approx 200 GPa, Y_{\text{bone}} \approx 20 GPa, Y_{\text{rubber}} \approx 0.05 GPa.

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: Guitar strings: Tightening a guitar string stretches it, increasing the tension. Because steel has a high Young's modulus, even a small increase in length requires a large increase in force — which is why fine-tuning is sensitive. If strings were made of rubber (low Y), they would stretch far too easily to tune accurately.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Hang a known mass from a thin steel wire (e.g., a guitar string). Measure its diameter d and initial length L, and measure the extension \Delta L under the load. Calculate Young's modulus: Y = FL/(A\,\Delta L) where A = \pi d^2/4.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: Liquids and gases have no shear modulus (they flow instead of shearing). They do have a bulk modulus — water is about 2.2 GPa and air is about 0.14 MPa. This difference in bulk modulus is why sound travels so much faster in water than in air.

quiz.question: A wire (Y = 2×10¹¹ Pa) of length 1 m and cross-section 1 mm² stretches 1 mm under a load. What is the load?
